**Lod** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): לוד, [fully vocalized](/wiki/Ktiv_menuqad "Ktiv menuqad"): לֹד), also known as **Lydda** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Λύδδα) and **Lidd** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): اللِّدّ, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Lidd*, or اللُّدّ, *al-Ludd*), is a city 15 km (9+1⁄2 mi) southeast of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") and 40 km (25 mi) northwest of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") in the [Central District](/wiki/Central_District_(Israel) "Central District (Israel)") of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). It is situated between the lower [Shephelah](/wiki/Shephelah "Shephelah") on the east and the [coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain") on the west. The city had a population of 94,189 in 2024.[[1]](#cite_note-Israelpopulations-1)

Lod has been inhabited since at least the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is mentioned a few times in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") and in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Between the 5th century BCE and up until the late [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") period, it was a prominent center for [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") scholarship and trade.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) Around 200 CE, the city became a [Roman colony](/wiki/Roman_colony "Roman colony") and was renamed *Diospolis* (Ancient Greek: Διόσπολις, lit. 'city of [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus")'). Tradition identifies Lod as the 4th century martyrdom site of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George");[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) the [Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_George_and_Mosque_of_Al-Khadr "Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr") located in the city is believed to have housed his remains.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Following the [Arab conquest of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant"), Lod served as the capital of [Jund Filastin](/wiki/Jund_Filastin "Jund Filastin"); however, a few decades later, the seat of power was transferred to [Ramla](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla"), and Lod slipped in importance.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) Under [Crusader](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") rule, the city was a Catholic [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and it remains a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see") to this day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Lod underwent a major change in its population in the mid-20th century.[[9]](#cite_note-Rabinowitz_Monterescu_2008-9) Exclusively Palestinian Arab in 1947,[[9]](#cite_note-Rabinowitz_Monterescu_2008-9) Lod was part of the area designated for an Arab state in the [United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine"); however, in July 1948, the city was occupied by the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces"), and most of its Arab inhabitants were expelled in the [Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle](/wiki/Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle").[[10]](#cite_note-Shapira-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The city was largely resettled by Jewish immigrants, most of them [expelled from Arab countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_lands "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim lands").[[12]](#cite_note-gibb-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Today, Lod is one of Israel's [mixed cities](/wiki/Mixed_cities "Mixed cities"), with an [Arab](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") population of 30%.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Lod is one of Israel's major transportation hubs. The main international airport, [Ben Gurion Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_Airport "Ben Gurion Airport"), is located 8 km (5 miles) north of the city. The city is also a major railway and road junction.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Religious references

The Hebrew name Lod appears in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") as a town of [Benjamin](/wiki/Tribe_of_Benjamin "Tribe of Benjamin"), founded along with [Ono](/wiki/Ono,_Benjamin "Ono, Benjamin") by Shamed or Shamer (1 Chronicles 8:12; Ezra 2:33; Nehemiah 7:37; 11:35). In [Ezra 2:33](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Ezra+2:33&version=nkjv), it is mentioned as one of the cities whose inhabitants [returned](/wiki/Return_to_Zion "Return to Zion") after the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"). Lod is not mentioned among the towns allocated to the tribe of Benjamin in [Joshua 18:11–28](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Joshua+18:11–28&version=nkjv).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The name Lod derives from a [tri-consonantal root](/wiki/Semitic_root "Semitic root") not extant in [Northwest Semitic](/wiki/Northwest_Semitic_languages "Northwest Semitic languages"), but only in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") (“to quarrel; withhold, hinder”). An Arabic etymology of such an ancient name is unlikely (the earliest attestation is from the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") period).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), the town appears in its Greek form, Lydda,[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) as the site of [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter")'s healing of [Aeneas](/wiki/Aeneas_(biblical_figure) "Aeneas (biblical figure)") in [Acts 9:32–38](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Acts#9:32 "s:Bible (King James)/Acts").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The city is also mentioned in an Islamic [hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith") as the location of the battlefield where the false messiah ([al-Masih ad-Dajjal](/wiki/Al-Masih_ad-Dajjal "Al-Masih ad-Dajjal")) will be slain before the [Day of Judgment](/wiki/Day_of_Judgment "Day of Judgment").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## History

### Neolithic and Chalcolithic

Further information: [Lodian culture](/wiki/Lodian_culture "Lodian culture")

The first occupation dates to the [Neolithic in the Near East](/wiki/Neolithic_in_the_Near_East "Neolithic in the Near East") and is associated with the [Lodian culture](/wiki/Lodian_culture "Lodian culture").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Brink-23) Occupation continued in the [Levant Chalcolithic](/wiki/Levant_Chalcolithic "Levant Chalcolithic").[[23]](#cite_note-Brink-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Pottery finds have dated the initial settlement in the area now occupied by the town to 5600–5250 BCE.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Early Bronze

In the Early Bronze, it was an important settlement in the central coastal plain between the Judean Shephelah and the Mediterranean coast, along Nahal Ayalon.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Other important nearby sites were Tel Dalit, Tel Bareqet, Khirbat Abu Hamid (Shoham North), [Tel Afeq](/wiki/Tel_Afek "Tel Afek"), [Azor](/wiki/Azor "Azor") and [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa").

Two architectural phases belong to the late EB I in Area B.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The first phase had a mudbrick wall, while the late phase included a circulat stone structure. Later excavations have produced an occupation later, Stratum IV.[[23]](#cite_note-Brink-23) It consists of two phases, Stratum IVb with mudbrick wall on stone foundations and rounded exterior corners. In Stratum IVa there was a mudbrick wall with no stone foundations, with imported Egyptian potter and local pottery imitations.

Another excavations revealed nine occupation strata. Strata VI-III belonged to Early Bronze IB. The material culture showed Egyptian imports in strata V and IV.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Occupation continued into Early Bronze II with four strata (V-II). There was continuity in the material culture and indications of centralized urban planning.

### Middle Bronze

North to the tell were scattered MB II burials.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Late Bronze

The earliest written record is in a list of [Canaanite](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") towns drawn up by the [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") [Thutmose III](/wiki/Thutmose_III "Thutmose III") at [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak") in [1465 BCE](/wiki/2nd_millennium_BC "2nd millennium BC").[[31]](#cite_note-Franciscan-31)

### Classical era

From the fifth century BCE until the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_period "Roman period"), the city was a centre of Jewish scholarship[[32]](#cite_note-32) and commerce.[[33]](#cite_note-EB-33)

According to British historian [Martin Gilbert](/wiki/Martin_Gilbert "Martin Gilbert"), during the [Hasmonean](/wiki/Hasmonean "Hasmonean") period, [Jonathan Maccabee](/wiki/Jonathan_Maccabee "Jonathan Maccabee") and his brother, [Simon Maccabaeus](/wiki/Simon_Maccabaeus "Simon Maccabaeus"), enlarged the area under Jewish control, which included conquering the city.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Roman era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Lidda_2789.jpg/250px-Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Lidda_2789.jpg)](/wiki/File:Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Lidda_2789.jpg)

Depiction of Lydda in the [Umm ar-Rasas mosaics](/wiki/Umm_ar-Rasas_mosaics "Umm ar-Rasas mosaics"), 8th century CE

The Jewish community in Lod during the Mishnah and Talmud era is described in a significant number of sources, including information on its institutions, demographics, and way of life. The city reached its height as a Jewish center between the [First Jewish-Roman War](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War") and the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt"), and again in the days of [Judah ha-Nasi](/wiki/Judah_ha-Nasi "Judah ha-Nasi") and the start of the [Amoraim](/wiki/Amoraim "Amoraim") period. The city was then the site of numerous public institutions, including schools, study houses, and synagogues.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

In 43 BC, [Cassius](/wiki/Gaius_Cassius_Longinus "Gaius Cassius Longinus"), the Roman governor of [Syria](/wiki/Roman_Syria "Roman Syria"), sold the inhabitants of Lod into slavery, but they were set free two years later by [Mark Antony](/wiki/Mark_Antony "Mark Antony").[[35]](#cite_note-CE-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

During the First Jewish–Roman War, the Roman *[proconsul](/wiki/Proconsul "Proconsul")* of Syria, [Cestius Gallus](/wiki/Cestius_Gallus "Cestius Gallus"), razed the town on his way to [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") in *[Tishrei](/wiki/Tishrei "Tishrei")* 66 CE. According to Josephus, "[he] found the city deserted, for the entire population had [gone up to Jerusalem](/wiki/Three_Pilgrimage_Festivals "Three Pilgrimage Festivals") for the [Feast of Tabernacles](/wiki/Sukkot "Sukkot"). He killed fifty people whom he found, burned the town and marched on".[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[37]](#cite_note-:6-37) Lydda was occupied by Emperor [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian") in 68 CE.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In the period following the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE, [Rabbi Tarfon](/wiki/Rabbi_Tarfon "Rabbi Tarfon"), who appears in many Tannaitic and Jewish legal discussions, served as a rabbinic authority in Lod.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

During the [Kitos War](/wiki/Kitos_War "Kitos War"), 115–117 CE, the Roman army laid siege to Lod, where the rebel Jews had gathered under the leadership of Julian and Pappos. Torah study was outlawed by the Romans and pursued mostly in the underground.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The distress became so great, the patriarch Rabban [Gamaliel II](/wiki/Gamaliel_II "Gamaliel II"), who was shut up there and died soon afterwards, permitted fasting on [Ḥanukkah](/wiki/%E1%B8%A4anukkah "Ḥanukkah"). Other rabbis disagreed with this ruling.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Lydda was next taken and many of the Jews were executed; the "slain of Lydda" are often mentioned in words of reverential praise in the Talmud.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 200 CE, emperor [Septimius Severus](/wiki/Septimius_Severus "Septimius Severus") elevated the town to the status of a city, calling it *Colonia Lucia Septimia Severa Diospolis*.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The name *Diospolis* ("City of Zeus") may have been bestowed earlier, possibly by Hadrian.[[44]](#cite_note-44) At that point, most of its inhabitants were [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"). The earliest known [bishop](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lydda "Bishop of Lydda") is Aëtius, a friend of [Arius](/wiki/Arius "Arius").[[35]](#cite_note-CE-35)

During the following century (200-300CE), it's said that [Joshua ben Levi](/wiki/Joshua_ben_Levi "Joshua ben Levi") founded a yeshiva in Lod.

### Byzantine period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/La_tomba_di_San_Giorgio_%28Lod%2C_Israele%29_02.JPG/250px-La_tomba_di_San_Giorgio_%28Lod%2C_Israele%29_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:La_tomba_di_San_Giorgio_(Lod,_Israele)_02.JPG)

Tomb of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"), first mentioned about 530 by the pilgrim Theodosius[[35]](#cite_note-CE-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Byzantine_floor_mosaic_map_at_St._George_Church_Madaba_P1090117.JPG/250px-Byzantine_floor_mosaic_map_at_St._George_Church_Madaba_P1090117.JPG)](/wiki/File:Byzantine_floor_mosaic_map_at_St._George_Church_Madaba_P1090117.JPG)

[Madaba Map](/wiki/Madaba_Map "Madaba Map"), 6th century CE, showing Lod above and left (NW) of the red "[ΚΛΗ]ΡΟϹ ΔΑΝ" ("the lot of Dan") inscription (left margin, touching on damaged area)

In December 415, the [Council of Diospolis](/wiki/Council_of_Diospolis "Council of Diospolis") was held here to try [Pelagius](/wiki/Pelagius_(British_monk) "Pelagius (British monk)"); he was acquitted. In the sixth century, the city was renamed Georgiopolis[[45]](#cite_note-45) after [St. George](/wiki/St._George "St. George"), a soldier in the guard of the emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"), who was born there between 256 and 285 CE.[[46]](#cite_note-Frenkel-46)

The [Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_George_and_Mosque_of_Al-Khadr "Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr") is named for him.[[31]](#cite_note-Franciscan-31)
The 6th-century [Madaba map](/wiki/Madaba_map "Madaba map") shows Lydda as an unwalled city with a cluster of buildings under a black inscription reading "Lod, also Lydea, also Diospolis".[[47]](#cite_note-47) An isolated large building with a semicircular colonnaded plaza in front of it might represent the St George shrine.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Early Muslim period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Haan_Lod.jpg/250px-Haan_Lod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haan_Lod.jpg)

Khan el-Hilu, Lod

After the [Muslim conquest of Palestine](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Palestine "Muslim conquest of Palestine") by [Amr ibn al-'As](/wiki/Amr_ibn_al-%27As "Amr ibn al-'As") in 636 CE,[[49]](#cite_note-49) Lod which was referred to as "al-Ludd" in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") served as the capital of [Jund Filastin](/wiki/Jund_Filastin "Jund Filastin") ("Military District of Palaestina") before the seat of power was moved to nearby [Ramla](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla") during the reign of the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") Caliph [Suleiman ibn Abd al-Malik](/wiki/Suleiman_ibn_Abd_al-Malik "Suleiman ibn Abd al-Malik") in 715–716. The population of al-Ludd was relocated to Ramla, as well.[[50]](#cite_note-50) With the relocation of its inhabitants and the construction of the [White Mosque](/wiki/White_Mosque_(Ramla) "White Mosque (Ramla)") in Ramla, al-Ludd lost its importance and fell into decay.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)

The city was visited by the local Arab geographer [al-Muqaddasi](/wiki/Al-Muqaddasi "Al-Muqaddasi") in 985, when it was under the [Fatimid Caliphate](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate"), and was noted for its Great Mosque which served the residents of al-Ludd, Ramla, and the nearby villages. He also wrote of the city's "wonderful church (of St. George) at the gate of which Christ will slay the [Antichrist](/wiki/Masih_ad-Dajjal "Masih ad-Dajjal")."[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Crusader and Ayyubid period

The [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") occupied the city in 1099 and named it St Jorge de Lidde.[[33]](#cite_note-EB-33) It was briefly conquered by [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin"), but retaken by the Crusaders in 1191. For the English [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), it was a place of great significance as the birthplace of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"). The Crusaders made it the seat of a [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") diocese,[[52]](#cite_note-52) and it remains a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see").[[35]](#cite_note-CE-35) It owed the service of 10 knights and 20 sergeants, and it had its own burgess court during this era.[[53]](#cite_note-Pringlep11-53)

In 1226, Ayyubid Syrian geographer [Yaqut al-Hamawi](/wiki/Yaqut_al-Hamawi "Yaqut al-Hamawi") visited al-Ludd and stated it was part of the Jerusalem District during [Ayyubid](/wiki/Ayyubid_dynasty "Ayyubid dynasty") rule.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Mamluk period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Konrad_von_Gr%C3%BCnenberg_-_Beschreibung_der_Reise_von_Konstanz_nach_Jerusalem_-_Blatt_33r_-_071.jpg/250px-Konrad_von_Gr%C3%BCnenberg_-_Beschreibung_der_Reise_von_Konstanz_nach_Jerusalem_-_Blatt_33r_-_071.jpg)](/wiki/File:Konrad_von_Gr%C3%BCnenberg_-_Beschreibung_der_Reise_von_Konstanz_nach_Jerusalem_-_Blatt_33r_-_071.jpg)

Lydda with ruined church over the tomb of St George and adjacent mosque ([Konrad von Grünenberg](/wiki/Konrad_von_Gr%C3%BCnenberg "Konrad von Grünenberg"), 1487

Sultan [Baybars](/wiki/Baybars "Baybars") brought Lydda again under Muslim control by 1267–8.[[55]](#cite_note-Petersen203-55) According to [Qalqashandi](/wiki/Ahmad_al-Qalqashandi "Ahmad al-Qalqashandi"), Lydda was an administrative centre of a [wilaya](/wiki/Wilaya "Wilaya") during the fourteenth and fifteenth century in the [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate_(Cairo) "Mamluk Sultanate (Cairo)") empire.[[55]](#cite_note-Petersen203-55) [Mujir al-Din](/wiki/Mujir_al-Din "Mujir al-Din") described it as a pleasant village with an active Friday mosque.[[55]](#cite_note-Petersen203-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) During this time, Lydda was a station on the [postal route between Cairo and Damascus](/wiki/Barid "Barid").[[55]](#cite_note-Petersen203-55)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Ottoman period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Lydda_between_1890_and_1900.jpg/250px-Lydda_between_1890_and_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lydda_between_1890_and_1900.jpg)

Lod, c.1890-1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Lydda_1903.jpg/250px-Lydda_1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lydda_1903.jpg)

Lydda, 1903

In 1517, Lydda was incorporated into the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") as part of the [Damascus Eyalet](/wiki/Damascus_Eyalet "Damascus Eyalet"), and in the 1550s, the revenues of Lydda were designated for the new [waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf") of [Hasseki Sultan Imaret](/wiki/Hasseki_Sultan_Imaret "Hasseki Sultan Imaret") in Jerusalem, established by Hasseki Hurrem Sultan ([Roxelana](/wiki/Roxelana "Roxelana")), the wife of [Suleiman the Magnificent](/wiki/Suleiman_the_Magnificent "Suleiman the Magnificent").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

By 1596 Lydda was a part of the *[nahiya](/wiki/Nahiya "Nahiya")* ("subdistrict") of [Ramla](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla"), which was under the administration of the *[liwa](/wiki/Liwa_(Arabic) "Liwa (Arabic)")* ("district") of [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"). It had a population of 241 households and 14 bachelors who were all Muslims, and 233 households who were Christians.[[59]](#cite_note-59) They paid a fixed tax-rate of 33,3 % on agricultural products, including wheat, barley, summer crops, vineyards, fruit trees, sesame, special product ("dawalib" =spinning wheels[[55]](#cite_note-Petersen203-55)), goats and beehives, in addition to occasional revenues and market toll, a total of 45,000 [Akçe](/wiki/Ak%C3%A7e "Akçe"). All of the revenue went to the [Waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf").[[60]](#cite_note-Hut_and_Abd-60)

In 1051 AH/1641/2, the Bedouin tribe of [al-Sawālima](/wiki/Al-Sawalima "Al-Sawalima") from around [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") attacked the villages of [Subṭāra](/wiki/Subtara "Subtara"), [Bayt Dajan](/wiki/Bayt_Dajan "Bayt Dajan"), [al-Sāfiriya](/wiki/Al-Safiriyya "Al-Safiriyya"), [Jindās](/wiki/Jindas "Jindas"), Lydda and [Yāzūr](/wiki/Yazur "Yazur") belonging to [Waqf Haseki Sultan](/wiki/Haseki_Sultan_Imaret "Haseki Sultan Imaret").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The village appeared as *Lydda*, though misplaced, on the map of [Pierre Jacotin](/wiki/Pierre_Jacotin "Pierre Jacotin") compiled in [1799](/wiki/French_campaign_in_Egypt_and_Syria "French campaign in Egypt and Syria").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Missionary [William M. Thomson](/wiki/William_McClure_Thomson "William McClure Thomson") visited Lydda in the mid-19th century, describing it as a "flourishing village of some 2,000 inhabitants, imbosomed in noble orchards of [olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive"), [fig](/wiki/Ficus "Ficus"), [pomegranate](/wiki/Pomegranate "Pomegranate"), [mulberry](/wiki/Mulberry "Mulberry"), [sycamore](/wiki/Sycamore "Sycamore"), and other trees, surrounded every way by a very fertile neighbourhood. The inhabitants are evidently industrious and thriving, and the whole country between this and Ramleh is fast being filled up with their flourishing orchards. Rarely have I beheld a rural scene more delightful than this presented in early harvest ... It must be seen, heard, and enjoyed to be appreciated."[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In 1869, the population of Ludd was given as: 55 Catholics, 1,940 "Greeks", 5 Protestants and 4,850 Muslims.[[64]](#cite_note-SWP252-64) In 1870, the Church of Saint George was rebuilt. In 1892, the first [railway station](/wiki/Railway_station "Railway station") in the entire region was established in the city.[[65]](#cite_note-Shahinp260-65) In the second half of the 19th century, Jewish merchants migrated to the city, but left after the [1921 Jaffa riots](/wiki/Jaffa_riots_(May_1921) "Jaffa riots (May 1921)").[[65]](#cite_note-Shahinp260-65)

In 1882, the [Palestine Exploration Fund](/wiki/Palestine_Exploration_Fund "Palestine Exploration Fund")'s *Survey of Western Palestine* described Lod as "A small town, standing among enclosure of prickly pear, and having fine olive groves around it, especially to the south. The [minaret](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") of the [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") is a very conspicuous object over the whole of the plain. The inhabitants are principally Moslim, though the place is the seat of a Greek bishop resident of Jerusalem. [The Crusading church](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_George_and_Mosque_of_Al-Khadr "Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr") has lately been restored, and is used by the Greeks. Wells are found in the gardens...."[[64]](#cite_note-SWP252-64)

### British Mandate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/%D7%9C%D7%95%D7%93_-_%D7%9E%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%94-JNF022399.jpeg/250px-%D7%9C%D7%95%D7%93_-_%D7%9E%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%94-JNF022399.jpeg)](/wiki/File:%D7%9C%D7%95%D7%93_-_%D7%9E%D7%A8%D7%90%D7%94-JNF022399.jpeg)

Lydda, 1920

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Lydda_1932.jpg/250px-Lydda_1932.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lydda_1932.jpg)

Lydda, 1932

From 1918, Lydda was under the administration of the [British Mandate in Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), as per a [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") decree that followed the [Great War](/wiki/Great_War "Great War"). During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the British set up supply posts in and around Lydda and its railway station, also building an airport that was renamed [Ben Gurion Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_Airport "Ben Gurion Airport") after the death of Israel's first prime minister in 1973.[[65]](#cite_note-Shahinp260-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

At the time of the [1922 census of Palestine](/wiki/1922_census_of_Palestine "1922 census of Palestine"), Lydda had a population of 8,103 inhabitants (7,166 Muslims, 926 Christians, and 11 Jews),[[67]](#cite_note-67) the Christians were 921 Orthodox, 4 Roman Catholics and 1 [Melkite](/wiki/Melkite "Melkite").[[68]](#cite_note-68) This had increased by the [1931 census](/wiki/1931_census_of_Palestine "1931 census of Palestine") to 11,250 (10,002 Muslims, 1,210 Christians, 28 Jews, and 10 Bahai), in a total of 2475 residential houses.[[69]](#cite_note-Census1931-69)

In 1938, Lydda had a population of 12,750.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In 1945, Lydda had a population of 16,780 (14,910 Muslims, 1,840 Christians, 20 Jews and 10 "other").[[71]](#cite_note-1945p30-71) Until 1948, Lydda was an Arab town with a population of around 20,000—18,500 Muslims and 1,500 Christians.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Monterescup16-73) In 1947, the United Nations proposed [dividing Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine") into two states, one [Jewish state](/wiki/Jewish_state "Jewish state") and one Arab; Lydda was to form part of the proposed Arab state.[[74]](#cite_note-Sadip91-74) In the [ensuing war](/wiki/1948_Arab-Israeli_war "1948 Arab-Israeli war"), Israel captured Arab towns outside the area the UN had allotted it, including Lydda.

In December 1947, thirteen Jewish passengers in a seven-car convoy to [Ben Shemen Youth Village](/wiki/Ben_Shemen_Youth_Village "Ben Shemen Youth Village") were ambushed and murdered.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-shavit-76)In a separate incident, three Jewish youths, two men and a woman were captured, then raped and murdered in a neighbouring village.[[76]](#cite_note-shavit-76) Their bodies were paraded in Lydda’s principal street.[[76]](#cite_note-shavit-76)

### State of Israel

Main article: [Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle](/wiki/Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Downtown_area_of_Lod%2C_Israel_00262.JPG/250px-Downtown_area_of_Lod%2C_Israel_00262.JPG)](/wiki/File:Downtown_area_of_Lod,_Israel_00262.JPG)

View of a Lod street, 2005

The [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") entered Lydda on 11 July 1948.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The following day, under the impression that it was under attack,[[78]](#cite_note-Tal311-78) the 3rd Battalion was ordered to shoot anyone "seen on the streets". According to Israel, 250 Arabs were killed. Other estimates are higher: Arab historian [Aref al Aref](/wiki/Aref_al_Aref "Aref al Aref") estimated 400, and [Nimr al Khatib](/wiki/Nimr_al_Khatib "Nimr al Khatib") 1,700.[[79]](#cite_note-Morris-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Morrisp205-80)

In 1948, the population rose to 50,000 during the [Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba"), as [Arab refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees") fleeing other areas made their way there.[[65]](#cite_note-Shahinp260-65) A key event was the [Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle](/wiki/Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle"), with the expulsion of 50,000-70,000 Palestinians from Lydda and [Ramle](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla") by the Israel Defense Forces. All but 700[[81]](#cite_note-81) to 1,056[[12]](#cite_note-gibb-12) were expelled by order of the Israeli high command, and forced to walk 17 km (10+1⁄2 mi) to the Jordanian [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") lines. Estimates of those who died from exhaustion and dehydration vary from a handful to 355.[[82]](#cite_note-here-82)[[83]](#cite_note-Holmesp64-83) The town was subsequently [sacked](/wiki/Looting "Looting") by the Israeli army.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Some scholars, including [Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé"), characterize this as [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing").[[85]](#cite_note-85) The few hundred Arabs who remained in the city were soon outnumbered by the influx of Jews who [immigrated](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") to Lod from August 1948 onward, most of them from Arab countries.[[12]](#cite_note-gibb-12) As a result, Lod became a predominantly Jewish town.[[73]](#cite_note-Monterescup16-73)[[86]](#cite_note-Yacobi29-86)

After the establishment of the state, the biblical name Lod was readopted.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

The Jewish immigrants who settled Lod came in waves, [first](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries") from [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") and [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"), later from [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), and then from the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Since 2008, many urban development projects have been undertaken to improve the image of the city. Upscale neighbourhoods have been built, among them Ganei Ya'ar and Ahisemah, expanding the city to the east. According to a 2010 report in the *Economist*, a three-meter-high wall was built between Jewish and Arab neighbourhoods and construction in Jewish areas was given priority over construction in Arab neighborhoods. The newspaper says that violent crime in the Arab sector revolves mainly around family feuds over turf and honour crimes.[[89]](#cite_note-Econ-89) In 2010, the Lod Community Foundation organised an event for representatives of bicultural youth movements, volunteer aid organisations, educational start-ups, businessmen, sports organizations, and conservationists working on programmes to better the city.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Disorders_in_Lod%2C_May_2021._VII.jpg/250px-Disorders_in_Lod%2C_May_2021._VII.jpg)](/wiki/File:Disorders_in_Lod,_May_2021._VII.jpg)

[Israeli forces](/wiki/Israel_Police "Israel Police") in Lod, 11 May 2021

In the [2021 Israel–Palestine crisis](/wiki/2021_Israel%E2%80%93Palestine_crisis "2021 Israel–Palestine crisis"), a state of emergency was declared in Lod after Arab rioting led to the death of an Israeli Jew.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The Mayor of Lod, Yair Revivio, urged [Prime Minister of Israel](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel") [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") to deploy [Israel Border Police](/wiki/Israel_Border_Police "Israel Border Police") to restore order in the city.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) This was the first time since 1966 that Israel had declared this kind of emergency lockdown.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-StateOfEmergency-95) International media noted that both Jewish and Palestinian mobs were active in Lod, but the "crackdown came for one side" only.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

## Demographics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/AlNurMosqueLodJune202023_02.jpg/250px-AlNurMosqueLodJune202023_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlNurMosqueLodJune202023_02.jpg)

Al Nur Mosque

In the 19th century and until the [Lydda Death March](/wiki/Lydda_Death_March "Lydda Death March"), Lod was an exclusively Muslim-Christian town, with an estimated 6,850 inhabitants, of whom approximately 2,000 (29%) were Christian.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

According to the [Israel Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Israel_Central_Bureau_of_Statistics "Israel Central Bureau of Statistics") (CBS), the population of Lod in 2010 was 69,500 people.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

According to the 2019 census, the population of Lod was 77,223, of which 53,581 people, comprising 69.4% of the city's population, were classified as "[Jews and Others](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews")", and 23,642 people, comprising 30.6% as "[Arab](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel")".[[1]](#cite_note-Israelpopulations-1)

## Education

According to CBS, 38 schools and 13,188 pupils are in the city. They are spread out as 26 elementary schools and 8,325 elementary school pupils, and 13 high schools and 4,863 high school pupils. About 52.5% of 12th-grade pupils were entitled to a matriculation certificate in 2001.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Reception hall, Ben Gurion International Airport

The airport and related industries are a major source of employment for the residents of Lod. Other important factories in the city are the communication equipment company "[Talard](/wiki/Telrad_Networks "Telrad Networks")", "Cafe-Co" - a subsidiary of the [Strauss Group](/wiki/Strauss_Group "Strauss Group") and "Kashev" - the computer center of [Bank Leumi](/wiki/Bank_Leumi "Bank Leumi").

A [Jewish Agency](/wiki/Jewish_Agency_for_Israel "Jewish Agency for Israel") Absorption Centre is also located in Lod. According to CBS figures for 2000, 23,032 people were salaried workers and 1,405 were self-employed. The mean monthly wage for a salaried worker was [NIS](/wiki/New_Israeli_Shekel "New Israeli Shekel") 4,754, a real change of 2.9% over the course of 2000. Salaried men had a mean monthly wage of NIS 5,821 (a real change of 1.4%) versus NIS 3,547 for women (a real change of 4.6%). The mean income for the self-employed was NIS 4,991. About 1,275 people were receiving unemployment benefits and 7,145 were receiving an income supplement.

## Art and culture

In 2009-2010, [Dor Guez](/wiki/Dor_Guez "Dor Guez") held an exhibit, *Georgeopolis*, at the [Petach Tikva](/wiki/Petach_Tikva "Petach Tikva") art museum that focuses on Lod.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

## Archaeology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Flickr_-_Government_Press_Office_%28GPO%29_-_Archeologists.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Government_Press_Office_%28GPO%29_-_Archeologists.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Government_Press_Office_(GPO)_-_Archeologists.jpg)

Archaeologists working on mosaic floor

A well-preserved mosaic floor dating to the Roman period was excavated in 1996 as part of a [salvage dig](/wiki/Rescue_archaeology "Rescue archaeology") conducted on behalf of the [Israel Antiquities Authority](/wiki/Israel_Antiquities_Authority "Israel Antiquities Authority") and the Municipality of Lod, prior to widening HeHalutz Street. According to
Jacob Fisch, executive director of the Friends of the Israel Antiquities Authority, a worker at the construction site noticed the tail of a tiger and halted work.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The mosaic was initially covered over with soil at the conclusion of the excavation for lack of funds to conserve and develop the site.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The mosaic is now part of the [Lod Mosaic Archaeological Center](/wiki/Lod_Mosaic_Archaeological_Center "Lod Mosaic Archaeological Center"). The floor, with its colorful display of birds, fish, exotic animals and merchant ships, is believed to have been commissioned by a wealthy resident of the city for his private home.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

The Lod Community Archaeology Program, which operates in ten Lod schools, five Jewish and five Israeli Arab, combines archaeological studies with participation in digs in Lod.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

## Sports

The city's major football club, [Hapoel Bnei Lod](/wiki/Hapoel_Bnei_Lod_F.C. "Hapoel Bnei Lod F.C."), plays in [Liga Leumit](/wiki/Liga_Leumit "Liga Leumit") (the second division). Its home is at the [Lod Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Lod_Municipal_Stadium "Lod Municipal Stadium"). The club was formed by a merger of Bnei Lod and Rakevet Lod in the 1980s. Two other clubs in the city play in the regional leagues: Hapoel MS Ortodoxim Lod in [Liga Bet](/wiki/Liga_Bet "Liga Bet") and Maccabi Lod in [Liga Gimel](/wiki/Liga_Gimel "Liga Gimel").

[Hapoel Lod](/wiki/Hapoel_Lod_F.C. "Hapoel Lod F.C.") played in the top division during the 1960s and 1980s, and won the [State Cup](/wiki/Israel_State_Cup "Israel State Cup") in 1984. The club folded in 2002. A new club, Hapoel Maxim Lod (named after former mayor [Maxim Levy](/wiki/Maxim_Levy "Maxim Levy")) was established soon after, but folded in 2007.
